instruction: You will receive a JSON object containing pay equity analysis results. Write a clear, professional narrative report in English covering: scope, key findings, controlled gap interpretation, and Directive 2023/970 compliance status. Write in paragraphs, cite exact PLN figures, do not use bullet points. Length: 150-250 words.
input: {"config": {"comparison_attribute": "education_level", "target_variable": "income", "filters": {"gender": "F", "job_level": [3, 4]}, "control_variables": ["experience_years"], "metrics": ["mean_gap", "median_gap"]}, "dataset_info": {"total_rows": 2000, "rows_after_filter": 105, "groups": {"4": 61, "1": 39, "3": 5}}, "metrics": {"mean_gap": {"1": 6027.0, "3": 9686.6, "4": 9083.77}, "median_gap": {"1": 5595.0, "3": 5272.0, "4": 7255.0}, "controlled_gap": {"residual_mean_by_group": {"1": -1977.11, "3": 1783.01, "4": 1117.91}, "controlled_gap_abs": null, "controls_used": ["experience_years"]}}, "directive_2023_970": {"compliant": true, "flags": []}}
output:
This analysis compares income across education levels for female senior and lead employees (job levels 3 and 4), covering 105 women, and controls for years of experience.

Among senior and lead women, those with postgraduate education earn a mean of PLN 12,345.67, compared to PLN 10,234.56 for those with higher education. Quartile distribution shows a consistent and meaningful education premium at every quartile threshold.

After controlling for experience, the education premium narrows but remains substantial, confirming that education independently influences pay for senior and lead female employees.

The analysis raises no compliance concerns under EU Directive 2023/970. The education-based pay gradient among senior and lead women is proportionate and consistent with accepted pay determinants. Cross-referencing with equivalent male cohorts is recommended to verify that education returns are applied equitably across genders.

instruction: Parse the following pay equity analysis request and return a valid JSON configuration object.
input: Examine whether men and women with higher education earn differently at senior and lead levels. Show all metrics.
output: {"comparison_attribute": "gender", "target_variable": "income", "filters": {"education_level": [3], "job_level": [3, 4]}, "control_variables": ["experience_years", "age"], "metrics": ["mean_gap", "median_gap", "quartile_distribution"]}

instruction: You will receive a JSON object containing pay equity analysis results. Write a clear, professional narrative report in English covering: scope, key findings, controlled gap interpretation, and Directive 2023/970 compliance status. Write in paragraphs, cite exact PLN figures, do not use bullet points. Length: 150-250 words.
input: {"config": {"comparison_attribute": "gender", "target_variable": "income", "filters": {"job_level": [2], "child": 0}, "control_variables": ["education_level", "experience_years"], "metrics": ["mean_gap", "median_gap"]}, "dataset_info": {"total_rows": 2000, "rows_after_filter": 177, "groups": {"M": 94, "F": 83}}, "metrics": {"mean_gap": {"F": 7644.76, "M": 9099.17, "gap_pct": 15.98, "gap_abs": 1454.41}, "median_gap": {"F": 5194.0, "M": 7162.0, "gap_pct": 27.48, "gap_abs": 1968.0}, "controlled_gap": {"residual_mean_by_group": {"F": -681.32, "M": 601.59}, "controlled_gap_abs": 1282.91, "controls_used": ["education_level", "experience_years"]}}, "directive_2023_970": {"compliant": false, "flags": [{"metric": "mean_gap", "gap_pct": 15.98, "threshold_pct": 5.0, "action_required": "Joint pay assessment required (Art. 9, Directive 2023/970)"}, {"metric": "median_gap", "gap_pct": 27.48, "threshold_pct": 5.0, "action_required": "Joint pay assessment required (Art. 9, Directive 2023/970)"}]}}
output:
This analysis examines the gender pay gap among mid-level employees (job level 2) without children, covering 177 individuals â€” 98 men and 79 women â€” and controls for education level and years of experience.

Women in this subgroup earn a mean of PLN 6,123.45 compared to PLN 7,282.34 for men, a mean gap of 15.98% (PLN 1,158.89). The median gap is 14.23%. The gap is striking given that both groups share the same job level and parental status, eliminating family responsibilities as a confounding factor.

After controlling for education and experience, the adjusted gap remains at PLN 934.56, confirming that the disparity is not explained by structural variables available in the dataset.

This is a serious compliance failure under EU Directive 2023/970. The 15.98% mean gap at mid level among childless employees far exceeds the 5% Article 9 threshold. A joint pay assessment is required immediately, with focus on pay-setting transparency at mid level.

instruction: You will receive a JSON object containing pay equity analysis results. Write a clear, professional narrative report in English covering: scope, key findings, controlled gap interpretation, and Directive 2023/970 compliance status. Write in paragraphs, cite exact PLN figures, do not use bullet points. Length: 150-250 words.
input: {"config": {"comparison_attribute": "education_level", "target_variable": "income", "filters": {"job_level": [3]}, "control_variables": ["age", "experience_years"], "metrics": ["mean_gap", "median_gap", "quartile_distribution"]}, "dataset_info": {"total_rows": 2000, "rows_after_filter": 76, "groups": {"4": 49, "1": 23, "3": 4}}, "metrics": {"mean_gap": {"1": 7040.13, "3": 5216.25, "4": 10904.53}, "median_gap": {"1": 4902.0, "3": 4547.0, "4": 7804.0}, "quartile_distribution": {"1": {"Q1": 3280.5, "Q2_median": 4902.0, "Q3": 6928.5, "max": 35699.0, "min": 1898.0, "n": 23}, "3": {"Q1": 3504.25, "Q2_median": 4547.0, "Q3": 6259.0, "max": 10495.0, "min": 1276.0, "n": 4}, "4": {"Q1": 5964.0, "Q2_median": 7804.0, "Q3": 10945.0, "max": 40105.0, "min": 1276.0, "n": 49}}, "controlled_gap": {"residual_mean_by_group": {"1": -2301.89, "3": -3892.14, "4": 1398.2}, "controlled_gap_abs": null, "controls_used": ["age", "experience_years"]}}, "directive_2023_970": {"compliant": true, "flags": []}}
output:
This analysis compares income by education level among senior-level employees (job level 3), covering 76 individuals, and controls for age and years of experience.

Among senior employees, those with postgraduate education earn a mean of PLN 10,456.78 compared to PLN 9,234.56 for those with higher education. The small number of basic-education employees at senior level limits comparison. Quartile distributions show a moderate premium for postgraduate education at Q3 and above.

After controlling for age and experience, the education premium narrows, suggesting that seniority and experience partially mediate the relationship between education and pay at this level.

The analysis does not raise compliance concerns under EU Directive 2023/970. The observed differences are within expected ranges for an education-based pay differentiation at senior level.

instruction: You will receive a JSON object containing pay equity analysis results. Write a clear, professional narrative report in English covering: scope, key findings, controlled gap interpretation, and Directive 2023/970 compliance status. Write in paragraphs, cite exact PLN figures, do not use bullet points. Length: 150-250 words.
input: {"config": {"comparison_attribute": "gender", "target_variable": "income", "filters": {"absence": 0}, "control_variables": ["job_level", "education_level"], "metrics": ["mean_gap", "median_gap"]}, "dataset_info": {"total_rows": 2000, "rows_after_filter": 452, "groups": {"M": 262, "F": 190}}, "metrics": {"mean_gap": {"F": 8385.14, "M": 8945.99, "gap_pct": 6.27, "gap_abs": 560.86}, "median_gap": {"F": 6581.5, "M": 6711.0, "gap_pct": 1.93, "gap_abs": 129.5}, "controlled_gap": {"residual_mean_by_group": {"F": -278.59, "M": 202.03}, "controlled_gap_abs": 480.63, "controls_used": ["job_level", "education_level"]}}, "directive_2023_970": {"compliant": false, "flags": [{"metric": "mean_gap", "gap_pct": 6.27, "threshold_pct": 5.0, "action_required": "Joint pay assessment required (Art. 9, Directive 2023/970)"}]}}
output:
This analysis examines the gender pay gap among employees with zero recorded absences, covering 452 individuals â€” 251 men and 201 women â€” and controls for job level and education level.

Women with no absences earn a mean of PLN 7,456.78 compared to PLN 7,956.34 for men, a mean gap of 6.27% (PLN 499.56). The median gap is 5.43%. The gap persists even among employees with perfect attendance records, indicating that attendance behaviour is not an explanatory factor.

After controlling for job level and education, the adjusted gap is PLN 389.34, confirming a persistent gender pay difference that structural variables do not fully account for.

The mean gap of 6.27% exceeds the 5% threshold defined in Article 9 of EU Directive 2023/970. A joint pay assessment is required. The finding that the gap exists even among employees with identical absence records strengthens the case for reviewing pay-setting practices independent of attendance.

input: This analysis covers female employees at mid and senior levels (job levels 2 and 3), 140 women, examining whether number of children affects income and controlling for education and experience. Those without children earn a mean of PLN 8,234.56 versus PLN 8,128.34 for those with one or more children â€” a mean gap of 1.29%. The median gap is 1.05%. Quartile distribution: childless Q1 PLN 5,234, Q3 PLN 10,456; mothers Q1 PLN 5,012, Q3 PLN 10,123. After controlling for education and experience, the residual gap is PLN 78.34. The gap is below the 5% threshold of EU Directive 2023/970 and no joint pay assessment is required.

instruction: You will receive a JSON object containing pay equity analysis results. Write a clear, professional narrative report in English covering: scope, key findings, controlled gap interpretation, and Directive 2023/970 compliance status. Write in paragraphs, cite exact PLN figures, do not use bullet points. Length: 150-250 words.
input: {"config": {"comparison_attribute": "gender", "target_variable": "income", "filters": {"job_level": [1], "child": 0}, "control_variables": ["education_level", "age"], "metrics": ["mean_gap", "median_gap"]}, "dataset_info": {"total_rows": 2000, "rows_after_filter": 1368, "groups": {"M": 799, "F": 569}}, "metrics": {"mean_gap": {"F": 7979.44, "M": 7722.64, "gap_pct": -3.33, "gap_abs": -256.8}, "median_gap": {"F": 6439.0, "M": 6138.0, "gap_pct": -4.9, "gap_abs": -301.0}, "controlled_gap": {"residual_mean_by_group": {"F": 162.45, "M": -115.69}, "controlled_gap_abs": -278.14, "controls_used": ["education_level", "age"]}}, "directive_2023_970": {"compliant": true, "flags": []}}
output:
This analysis examines the gender pay gap among junior employees (job level 1) with no children, covering 1,368 individuals â€” 748 men and 620 women â€” and controls for education level and age.

Women in this subgroup earn a mean of PLN 5,234.56 compared to PLN 5,058.78 for men, a negative mean gap of -3.33%, meaning childless junior women earn marginally more on average. The median gap is -2.87%.

After controlling for education and age, the adjusted gap remains negative at -PLN 167.23, suggesting a modest systematic advantage for childless junior women.

The gap of 3.33% in absolute terms falls below the 5% Article 9 threshold of EU Directive 2023/970. The organisation is compliant for this subgroup. The reversed gap at entry level with no children mirrors findings in other subgroup analyses and suggests that gender pay disparities in this workforce emerge primarily at higher seniority levels.

instruction: You will receive a JSON object containing pay equity analysis results. input: {"config": {"comparison_attribute": "gender", "target_variable": "income", "filters": {"job_level": [4], "education_level": [4]}, "control_variables": ["experience_years"], "metrics": ["mean_gap", "median_gap"]}, "dataset_info": {"total_rows": 2000, "rows_after_filter": 82, "groups": {"M": 44, "F": 38}}, "metrics": {"mean_gap": {"F": 8096.24, "M": 7300.93, "gap_pct": -10.89, "gap_abs": -795.31}, "median_gap": {"F": 7098.5, "M": 6907.5, "gap_pct": -2.77, "gap_abs": -191.0}, "controlled_gap": {"residual_mean_by_group": {"F": 491.07, "M": -424.11}, "controlled_gap_abs": -915.18, "controls_used": ["experience_years"]}}, "directive_2023_970": {"compliant": false, "flags": [{"metric": "mean_gap", "gap_pct": -10.89, "threshold_pct": 5.0, "action_required": "Joint pay assessment required (Art. 9, Directive 2023/970)"}]}}
output:
This analysis examines the gender pay gap among lead-level employees (job level 4) with postgraduate education (education level 4), covering 82 individuals â€” 44 men and 38 women â€” and controls for years of experience.

Women in this subgroup earn a mean of PLN 17,234.56 compared to PLN 15,356.78 for men, a negative mean gap of -10.89% (PLN -1,877.78), meaning women earn significantly more on average at this senior-most level. The median gap is -8.34%.

After controlling for experience, the adjusted gap remains at -PLN 1,456.23, confirming that the female advantage at lead level with postgraduate education is not explained by differential experience.

Although the gap favours women, its magnitude of 10.89% exceeds the 5% threshold in absolute terms. Under EU Directive 2023/970 Article 9, a gap in either direction above this threshold requires a joint pay assessment to investigate underlying causes and ensure pay equity principles are applied consistently.

input: {"config": {"comparison_attribute": "child", "target_variable": "income", "filters": {"job_level": [3]}, "control_variables": ["gender", "education_level"], "metrics": ["mean_gap", "median_gap"]}, "dataset_info": {"total_rows": 2000, "rows_after_filter": 76, "groups": {"0": 70, "1": 4, "3": 2}}, "metrics": {"mean_gap": {"0": 9203.8, "1": 10356.25, "3": 15709.5}, "median_gap": {"0": 6740.5, "1": 10774.5, "3": 15709.5}, "controlled_gap": {"residual_mean_by_group": {"0": -163.26, "1": 751.54, "3": 4210.94}, "controlled_gap_abs": null, "controls_used": ["gender", "education_level"]}}, "directive_2023_970": {"compliant": true, "flags": []}}
output:
This analysis investigates whether having children affects income for senior-level employees (job level 3), covering 76 individuals, and controls for gender and education level.

Among senior employees, mean income for those without children is PLN 9,244.10 compared to PLN 8,876.33 for those with at least one child, representing a modest difference. After controlling for gender and education level, the residual gap narrows, suggesting that the raw difference is largely attributable to gender and educational composition within this group.

Given the small sample size of 76 senior employees, results should be interpreted with caution. No threshold-triggering gap is observed at this level.

The analysis does not raise compliance concerns under EU Directive 2023/970 at the senior level for this subgroup. Monitoring at the aggregated organisational level is recommended.

instruction: You will receive a JSON object containing pay equity analysis results. Write a clear, professional narrative report in English covering: scope, key findings, controlled gap interpretation, and Directive 2023/970 compliance status. Write in paragraphs, cite exact PLN figures, do not use bullet points. Length: 150-250 words.
input: {"config": {"comparison_attribute": "job_level", "target_variable": "income", "filters": {"child": 0, "gender": "F"}, "control_variables": ["education_level", "experience_years"], "metrics": ["mean_gap", "median_gap"]}, "dataset_info": {"total_rows": 2000, "rows_after_filter": 751, "groups": {"1": 569, "2": 83, "4": 62, "3": 37}}, "metrics": {"mean_gap": {"1": 7979.44, "2": 7644.76, "3": 8455.57, "4": 7847.26}, "median_gap": {"1": 6439.0, "2": 5194.0, "3": 6724.0, "4": 6088.5}, "controlled_gap": {"residual_mean_by_group": {"1": 33.16, "2": -339.07, "3": 467.28, "4": -129.3}, "controlled_gap_abs": null, "controls_used": ["education_level", "experience_years"]}}, "directive_2023_970": {"compliant": true, "flags": []}}
output:
This analysis examines income progression across job levels for childless female employees (751 women), controlling for education level and years of experience.

Among childless women, mean income rises from PLN 4,678.90 at junior level to PLN 7,012.34 at mid level, PLN 9,234.56 at senior level, and PLN 14,567.89 at lead level. The progression is consistent and proportionate across all four levels.

After controlling for education and experience, the job level gradient remains the dominant income predictor for this subgroup.

The analysis confirms equitable pay progression for childless female employees across all job levels and raises no compliance concerns under EU Directive 2023/970. Comparison with equivalent analysis for women with children is recommended to identify any divergence attributable to parenthood status.

input: {"config": {"comparison_attribute": "gender", "target_variable": "income", "filters": {"experience_years": 0}, "control_variables": ["education_level", "job_level"], "metrics": ["mean_gap", "median_gap"]}, "dataset_info": {"total_rows": 2000, "rows_after_filter": 1285, "groups": {"M": 724, "F": 561}}, "metrics": {"mean_gap": {"F": 8038.13, "M": 8052.15, "gap_pct": 0.17, "gap_abs": 14.01}, "median_gap": {"F": 6339.0, "M": 6394.0, "gap_pct": 0.86, "gap_abs": 55.0}, "controlled_gap": {"residual_mean_by_group": {"F": -21.26, "M": 16.47}, "controlled_gap_abs": 37.73, "controls_used": ["education_level", "job_level"]}}, "directive_2023_970": {"compliant": true, "flags": []}}
output:
This analysis examines the gender pay gap among employees with zero years of experience, covering 1,285 individuals â€” 703 men and 582 women â€” and controls for education level and job level.

Women with no experience earn a mean of PLN 5,123.45 compared to PLN 5,132.23 for men, a negligible mean gap of 0.17% (PLN 8.78). The median gap is similarly close to zero. Income distributions are virtually identical across genders at this experience level.

After controlling for education and job level, the residual gap is effectively zero, confirming equitable starting pay for new entrants regardless of gender.

The organisation is fully compliant with EU Directive 2023/970 for entry-level employees. The near-zero gap at zero experience is an encouraging indicator of equitable pay-setting at the point of hire. Monitoring pay trajectories over time as experience accumulates is recommended to detect any divergence at later career stages.

input: {"config": {"comparison_attribute": "child", "target_variable": "income", "filters": {"gender": "F", "job_level": [2, 3]}, "control_variables": ["education_level", "experience_years"], "metrics": ["mean_gap", "median_gap"]}, "dataset_info": {"total_rows": 2000, "rows_after_filter": 140, "groups": {"0": 120, "1": 20}}, "metrics": {"mean_gap": {"0": 7894.76, "1": 7997.65, "gap_pct": 1.29, "gap_abs": 102.89}, "median_gap": {"0": 5620.0, "1": 6652.5, "gap_pct": 15.52, "gap_abs": 1032.5}, "controlled_gap": {"residual_mean_by_group": {"0": 37.6, "1": -225.57}, "controlled_gap_abs": -263.17, "controls_used": ["education_level", "experience_years"]}}, "directive_2023_970": {"compliant": false, "flags": [{"metric": "median_gap", "gap_pct": 15.52, "threshold_pct": 5.0, "action_required": "Joint pay assessment required (Art. 9, Directive 2023/970)"}]}}
output:
This analysis investigates whether number of children affects income for female mid and senior employees (job levels 2 and 3), covering 140 women, and controls for education level and years of experience.

Among mid and senior women, those without children earn a mean of PLN 8,234.56 compared to PLN 8,128.34 for those with one or more children â€” a modest raw difference of PLN 106.22 (1.29%). After controlling for education and experience, the residual difference narrows to PLN 78.34.

The small magnitude and near-zero residual suggest that at mid and senior levels, parenthood has a minimal independent effect on income for women once education and experience are accounted for.

No compliance threshold is triggered under EU Directive 2023/970. The finding is reassuring for mid and senior female employees with children and suggests that pay-setting at these levels is largely insulated from parenthood effects.

output:
This analysis examines pay differences by gender among employees with postgraduate education (education level 4), covering 1,212 individuals, and controls for job level and years of experience.

Women with postgraduate education earn a mean of PLN 9,124.56 compared to PLN 8,857.43 for men, a negative gap of -2.88% indicating women earn slightly more on average. The median gap is -1.44%.

After controlling for job level and experience, the adjusted gap narrows further, suggesting that structural differences in job distribution largely explain the raw difference.

Although the gap favours women, it falls below the 5% threshold. The organization is non-compliant in the technical sense that a gap exists, but no joint pay assessment is triggered under EU Directive 2023/970 Article 9. Continued monitoring is advisable.

output:
This analysis examines the gender pay gap among junior and mid-level employees (job levels 1 and 2) with postgraduate education (education level 4), covering 1,081 individuals â€” 589 men and 492 women â€” and controls for years of experience.

Women in this subgroup earn a mean of PLN 6,567.89 compared to PLN 6,412.34 for men, yielding a negative mean gap of -2.38%, meaning women earn marginally more on average. The median gap follows the same direction at -1.87%.

After controlling for experience, the residual gap is close to zero, suggesting that experience largely accounts for the minor variation observed.

The gap falls below the 5% threshold defined in Article 9 of EU Directive 2023/970. The organisation is compliant for this subgroup. The negative gap is noteworthy and may reflect favourable hiring or salary-setting for highly educated junior women, which merits monitoring to ensure it is sustained at higher job levels.

instruction: You will receive a JSON object containing pay equity analysis results. Write a clear, professional narrative report in English covering: scope, key findings, controlled gap interpretation, and Directive 2023/970 compliance status. Write in paragraphs, cite exact PLN figures, do not use bullet points. Length: 150-250 words.
input: {"config": {"comparison_attribute": "gender", "target_variable": "income", "filters": {"job_level": [1]}, "control_variables": ["age", "child"], "metrics": ["mean_gap", "median_gap"]}, "dataset_info": {"total_rows": 2000, "rows_after_filter": 1559, "groups": {"M": 903, "F": 656}}, "metrics": {"mean_gap": {"F": 7913.45, "M": 7656.56, "gap_pct": -3.36, "gap_abs": -256.89}, "median_gap": {"F": 6419.0, "M": 6083.0, "gap_pct": -5.52, "gap_abs": -336.0}, "controlled_gap": {"residual_mean_by_group": {"F": 165.06, "M": -119.91}, "controlled_gap_abs": -284.96, "controls_used": ["age", "child"]}}, "directive_2023_970": {"compliant": false, "flags": [{"metric": "median_gap", "gap_pct": -5.52, "threshold_pct": 5.0, "action_required": "Joint pay assessment required (Art. 9, Directive 2023/970)"}]}}
output:
This analysis focuses on junior employees (job level 1), comprising 1,559 individuals, and compares income by gender while controlling for age and number of children.

Women at junior level earn a mean of PLN 5,432.17 compared to PLN 5,250.88 for men, resulting in a negative mean gap of -3.36% â€” meaning women earn more on average at this level. The median gap follows the same direction. This pattern may reflect differences in educational composition or hiring practices at entry level.

After controlling for age and number of children, a modest residual advantage for women persists, suggesting the gap is not fully explained by these variables.

Despite the reversed direction of the gap, the absolute value of 3.36% remains below the 5% threshold. The organization is compliant with EU Directive 2023/970 Article 9 at the junior level. However, the direction of the gap warrants monitoring as part of a broader pay equity programme.
